Name: info_theoretic_quantization
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Model Compression and Quantization
Short Hypothesis: The compression limit of deep learning models can be reframed as a model- and task-conditioned quantity rather than a fixed property of the model, by leveraging information-theoretic principles and the concept of rate-distortion theory.
Related Work: Recent works such as EMQ, AMP-ViT, and SNIP have explored mixed-precision quantization and compression techniques for deep learning models. However, these approaches often rely on heuristic-based methods and lack a clear understanding of the underlying information-theoretic limits. In contrast, our proposal seeks to establish a theoretical framework for model compression and quantization, grounded in principles from coding theory and statistics.
Abstract: This research proposal aims to investigate the information-theoretic limits of model compression and quantization in deep learning. By framing compression as a rate-distortion problem, we seek to derive fundamental bounds on the bits-per-weight required to preserve predictive sufficiency. We will explore the use of entropy estimators, mutual-information lower bounds, and Fisher-information sensitivities to allocate bits where they reduce task-relevant distortion most. Our goal is to establish a theoretical framework for model compression and quantization, which can inform the development of more efficient and effective compression techniques.
Experiments: Derive achievability and converse bounds for model compression and quantization using rate-distortion theory
Evaluate the performance of entropy estimators and mutual-information lower bounds in guiding bit allocation
Investigate the trade-off between compression ratio and task performance using distortion-rate curves
Compare the efficiency of different compression techniques, such as quantization and pruning, using information-theoretic metrics
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of deriving tight information-theoretic bounds for deep learning models
The challenge of developing efficient algorithms for computing entropy estimators and mutual-information lower bounds
The potential for limited generalizability of our results to different model architectures and tasks
